cues.x: How to Make Money in Stocks, by William O'Neil.
Picture this.
You're driving, or maybe you're grinding through a workout, and you want to walk away actually understanding one of the most influential books ever written about picking winning stocks.
Not a vague, motivational version of it, but the real machinery, the rules, the numbers, the way the author actually thought.
That's what we're going to do together over the next stretch of time.
I'm going to walk you through William O'Neil's system, the one he called CAN SLIM, in real depth, the way a mentor would if you had him captive in the passenger seat on a long drive.
And before we go a single step further, let me be clear about one thing.
This is a summary of O'Neil's method, his ideas, his rules.
It is not personalized financial advice, and investing in stocks carries real risk of losing money.
What I want to give you is a genuine understanding of how this man thought the market works, so you can evaluate it for yourself with your eyes open.
So let's start with who this guy was and why anyone should listen.
William O'Neil was a stockbroker who got obsessed with a simple question.
Why do some stocks go up five hundred, a thousand, two thousand percent, while most just wander sideways or sink?
Instead of guessing, or listening to opinions, he did something almost nobody bothered to do.
He went back and studied the biggest winning stocks in market history, decade after decade, going back to the late eighteen hundreds and running through the whole twentieth century.
He pulled the greatest winners of each era, the stocks that made fortunes, and he laid them side by side and asked, what did these have in common in the months right before they launched?
What did their earnings look like?
What were their charts doing?
Who was buying them?
He built what he called model books, essentially detailed profiles of every huge winner, the fundamentals and the price action together.
And out of that historical detective work, he found that the big winners were not random.
They shared a set of common traits before they made their giant moves.
Seven traits, actually.
And he turned those seven traits into an acronym so you could remember them.
C-A-N-S-L-I-M. CAN SLIM.

He later founded the newspaper Investor's Business Daily to give ordinary investors the kind of data he'd been using, the earnings numbers and relative strength figures that Wall Street kept to itself.
So this isn't a theory somebody dreamed up in an armchair.
It's a pattern pulled out of a hundred years of the market's actual biggest winners.
Let's go through all seven letters, slowly and properly, because each one is a real, usable idea.
The C stands for current quarterly earnings.
This is where O'Neil starts, and it's the beating heart of the fundamental side of his system.

When he studied the great winners, he found that in the quarter or two right before their huge price advances, their most recent quarterly earnings per share were up dramatically compared to the same quarter a year earlier.
Not up a little.
Up a lot.

His baseline rule of thumb was that current quarterly earnings per share should be up at least twenty-five percent versus the same quarter the year before, and in the very best stocks, the ones that became the legends, that number was often far higher, forty percent, a hundred percent, two hundred percent, sometimes more.
Now, why compare to the same quarter a year ago and not to last quarter?
Because many businesses are seasonal.
A retailer makes a fortune in the holiday quarter and less the rest of the year, so comparing this winter to last summer would be meaningless.
You compare like to like, this spring to last spring.
That's the year-over-year comparison, and it's the honest way to see whether the business is genuinely growing.
But O'Neil adds a subtler, more powerful idea on top of that, and this is the one most people miss.
He wanted to see acceleration.
It's not enough that earnings are up twenty-five percent.
He wanted the rate of growth to be speeding up over the last several quarters.
Imagine a company that grows earnings ten percent, then eighteen percent, then thirty-five percent, then sixty percent in successive quarters.
That accelerating pattern is a flashing signal that something big is happening inside the business, that demand is catching fire.
That acceleration is far more exciting than a company that grows a steady twenty percent every quarter like clockwork.
And on the flip side, he warned you to watch for deceleration in a stock you already own.

When a hot growth stock that was posting seventy, eighty percent earnings growth suddenly slows to twenty, even if twenty still sounds good, that slowdown is often an early warning that the stock's best days may be behind it.
A couple of practical cautions he throws in here.
Be a little skeptical of earnings growth driven by one-time events, like selling a building or a tax windfall, because that's not the engine repeating.
And look at whether sales, the actual revenue, are growing alongside earnings, ideally sales up at least twenty-five percent too, or accelerating.
A company can juice earnings for a while by cutting costs, but you can't cut your way to greatness forever.
Real winners have both the top line and the bottom line moving up together.
Now the A.
The A stands for annual earnings growth, and this is the longer-lens companion to the C.
The current quarter tells you what's happening right now, but you also want proof that this isn't a one-quarter fluke, that you're dealing with a genuinely excellent company with a track record.
So O'Neil looked for meaningful annual earnings growth over each of the last several years, roughly the last three years or so, with each year's annual earnings higher than the year before.
His rough benchmark was annual growth rates of at least twenty-five percent a year, and again, the bigger and the more consistent, the better.
He wanted to see a stairway climbing up, year after year, not a jagged saw-tooth of up one year, down the next.
The current quarter should be strong and the multi-year record should be strong.
When both line up, you've got a company with real momentum and real staying power underneath it.
There's a second measure O'Neil folds into the A, and it's an important one for judging quality.
Return on equity, or ROE.
This is a measure of how efficiently a company turns the shareholders' money into profit.
He liked to see a return on equity of at least seventeen percent.

A high ROE tells you management is genuinely good at deploying capital, that the company isn't just growing by constantly borrowing money or selling new shares, but is actually generating strong profit on the base it has.
Great companies tend to have high returns on equity.
It's a marker of a well-run, competitively strong business.

So the A, in full, is a strong multi-year record of rising annual earnings, ideally twenty-five percent a year or better, backed by a healthy return on equity of seventeen percent or more, telling you the growth is real, durable, and well-managed, not a fluke and not a mirage.
Next comes the N, and the N is genuinely one of the richest letters, because it's really two ideas bundled together, and the second one is deeply counterintuitive.
The N stands for something new.
When O'Neil studied his winners, he found that almost every single one of them had something new driving the company at the moment it took off.
A new product or a new service that was flying off the shelves.

Think of a company that invents a breakthrough gadget everyone suddenly wants, or a restaurant chain with a new concept that's expanding into city after city, or a drug company with a newly approved medicine.
Or it could be new management, a fresh leader who comes in and turns a sleepy company around.
Or new conditions in the industry, like a change in regulations, a shortage that sends prices soaring, or a new technology that transforms an entire sector.
Something new is almost always the fundamental spark, the fuel behind the fire.
In his research, an overwhelming majority of the biggest winners, he found something like ninety-five percent of them, had one of these new factors right before their run.
The lesson is simple.
Look for companies riding a genuine wave of change, doing something the market hasn't fully appreciated yet.
But here's the twist, the part that trips up almost every beginner.
The other new that O'Neil insisted on is a new high in the stock's price.
This is the paradox at the center of his whole philosophy, and it goes against every instinct you have.
Most people want to buy a stock that's cheap, that's fallen a long way, that looks like a bargain near its lows.
It feels safe.
It feels smart.
O'Neil says that instinct is exactly backwards, and his historical study proved it to him again and again.
The great winning stocks did not launch their huge moves from the bargain bin.
They launched when they were making new highs, when they were breaking out to prices they'd never seen before, emerging from a proper base of consolidation.
As he put it, what seems too high and risky to most people usually goes higher, and what seems low and cheap usually goes lower.
Think about why that's true.
A stock hitting a new high has no overhead resistance, nobody sitting on a loss waiting to sell out and get even the moment it comes back to their price.
Everyone who owns it is happy, it's in strong hands, and the new high is telling you something real, that demand is overwhelming supply, that the market has recognized this company's new story.
Meanwhile, a stock scraping along near its lows is usually cheap for a reason.
Something is wrong.
The falling knife keeps falling.

So O'Neil trains you to do the psychologically hard thing, to buy strength, not weakness, to buy the stock making bold new highs out of a sound base rather than the sad, cheap laggard everyone else is bottom-fishing.
We'll come back to exactly what a proper base looks like when we get to the charts, because the new high has to come out of the right kind of setup.
Now the S.
The S stands for supply and demand.
At its core, a stock's price is governed by the same force that governs the price of anything, supply and demand.
And O'Neil's insight here is about the supply of shares.
When a company has a huge number of shares outstanding, billions of them, it takes an enormous flood of buying to move that price, because there's so much stock floating around.
It's like trying to move an aircraft carrier.
But a company with a smaller number of shares outstanding, a smaller float, can move much faster and further on the same amount of buying, because the supply is tight.
When strong demand meets a limited supply of shares, the price can rocket.
So all else being equal, he had a preference for stocks with a smaller or more reasonable number of shares outstanding, because they have more upside horsepower.
He especially loved companies where management themselves owned a big chunk of the stock, because their interests are aligned with yours and it keeps the effective supply tight.
Now, don't misread this.
A big company with lots of shares can absolutely still be a great winner, and O'Neil isn't telling you to only buy tiny obscure companies.
What he's really teaching you here is to read volume, because volume is how you see demand with your own eyes.

When a stock breaks out to that new high we just talked about, O'Neil wants to see it happen on a big surge in trading volume, ideally volume at least forty to fifty percent above its normal average, and often much more.
That volume spike is the fingerprint of big money pouring in, real institutional demand confirming the move.
A breakout on weak, thin volume is suspect, it might be a head-fake.
A breakout on a massive volume surge is the market shouting that demand is real.
He also liked to see a company buying back its own shares in the open market, because that shrinks the supply and signals that management believes the stock is undervalued.
And he was wary of companies constantly issuing tons of new stock, diluting existing shareholders and flooding the market with supply.
So the S, in a sentence, is watch the supply of shares and read demand through volume.
Tight supply plus surging volume on the breakout is the sweet spot.
The L stands for leader or laggard, and this is about being ruthlessly picky about which stock you buy within a hot area of the market.
O'Neil's rule is blunt.
Buy the leaders and avoid the laggards.
In any strong industry group, there are usually one, two, maybe three companies that are the true leaders, the best performers, the ones with the strongest earnings and the strongest stock action.
And then there's a pack of weaker, cheaper me-too companies trailing behind them.
The natural temptation, again, is to reach for the cheaper laggard.
You think, well, the leader has already run up so much, surely the cheaper one in the same industry will catch up, and it's a better bargain.
O'Neil says no, that's a trap.
Over and over, the laggards that look like bargains dramatically underperform the true leaders.
The leader is the leader for a reason.
And how do you measure which is the leader, objectively, not just by gut?
This is where one of O'Neil's signature tools comes in, relative price strength.
Relative strength measures how a stock's price performance stacks up against all other stocks in the market over the past year.

In his framework it's expressed as a relative strength rating from one to ninety-nine, where a rating of, say, eighty means the stock has outperformed eighty percent of all other stocks over the last twelve months.
O'Neil wanted the stocks he bought to have high relative strength, generally a rating of eighty or better, and for the real market leaders often ninety and up.
You want to own the strongest horse in the strongest race, the stock that's already proving it's outrunning the crowd.
Buying a stock with weak relative strength, one that's lagging the market, just because it feels cheaper, is exactly the mistake he's warning against.
He talked about avoiding what he called sympathy plays, those weaker stocks in a group that move up a bit in sympathy with the real leader but never truly perform.
Own the general, not the stragglers.

And a related tip, once you own a leader and the group is running, if that leader starts badly breaking down while the group is still okay, pay attention, because the leaders often top first and warn you of trouble coming for the whole group.
The I stands for institutional sponsorship, and this one is about the big money, the mutual funds, the pension funds, the hedge funds, the professional investors who move billions.
Here's the reality O'Neil hammers home.
It's the buying power of these giant institutions that actually drives a stock's price up in a sustained way.
Retail investors like you and me buying a few hundred shares don't move a stock.
But a major fund building a multi-million-share position over weeks and months, that's the fuel that propels a stock hundreds of percent.

So O'Neil wanted to see that a stock had institutional sponsorship, that a reasonable number of quality institutional investors owned it and, crucially, that the number of funds owning it was increasing over recent quarters.
That growing sponsorship is the demand, the fuel, being poured on the fire.
He paid special attention to whether some of the better-performing, smarter fund managers were among the owners, because their presence is a vote of confidence from people who do deep homework.
But, and this is the balancing counterintuitive point, you do not want a stock that is over-owned, one that every single institution on earth already holds.
Think about it.
If a stock is already in every fund's portfolio, who's left to buy it?
All the demand is exhausted.
There's nobody left to push it higher, and if anything goes wrong, all those big holders rushing for the exit at once can crush the price.

So the sweet spot O'Neil describes is a stock with some solid, increasing institutional sponsorship, enough that smart money has discovered it and is fueling it, but not so much that it's a crowded, over-owned, played-out name that everybody and their grandmother already holds.
You want to be relatively early, buying alongside the smart money as they're building their positions, not late to a party that's already ending.
So the I is you want quality funds buying and their numbers growing, that's your fuel, but you want to avoid the over-owned stock where the buying power is already spent.
And now we arrive at the M, and I want you to lean in here, because O'Neil considered this the single most important letter in the entire acronym.
The M stands for market direction, the direction of the general market as a whole.
And here's the statistic that makes it so vital.
O'Neil found that roughly three out of every four stocks, about seventy-five percent, follow the direction of the overall market.
So you can do everything else perfectly.

You can find a company with explosive current earnings, a stellar annual record, an exciting new product, tight supply, clear leadership, growing institutional sponsorship, a picture-perfect chart, and if you buy it when the general market has rolled over into a downtrend, into a correction or a bear market, it will very likely get dragged down anyway with everything else.
Three out of four stocks sink when the tide goes out.
This is why O'Neil says you must, absolutely must, learn to read the direction of the overall market, and only be aggressively buying when the market itself is in a confirmed uptrend.
Getting this one letter right is what protects you from catastrophic losses, because the biggest damage always happens in bear markets.
He's blunt about it.
Most investors don't know how to interpret what the general market is doing, and it costs them dearly.
So how do you read the market's direction, in O'Neil's method?
You do it by watching the major market indexes every single day, the big benchmarks, and paying incredibly close attention to the relationship between their price movement and their trading volume.
Price and volume together, that's the language the market speaks.
Let me give you the two key signals he taught, because they're precise and usable.
The first is what he called a distribution day.
A distribution day is a day when a major index closes lower than the day before, and it does so on higher trading volume than the day before.
Think about what that means.
The market fell, and it fell on heavier volume, which tells you the big institutions were selling, distributing their shares, unloading.
One distribution day is just noise.

But when you see them cluster, when you count something like three, four, five distribution days piling up within a stretch of just two or three weeks, that's a warning that the big money is quietly heading for the exits, and it often signals that a market uptrend is topping and about to roll over.
When you see that distribution stacking up, O'Neil says it's time to get cautious, to stop buying, to raise cash, to protect yourself.
And the opposite signal, the beautiful one, is what he named the follow-through day, and this is his tool for spotting the birth of a brand new uptrend after a market decline.
Here's the scenario.
The market has been falling, you're in a correction or a bear market, everything looks grim.
At some point the market will try to bounce, it'll have an up day, an attempted rally.
But O'Neil learned that most first bounces fail and fizzle out, so he taught you not to jump in on day one.
Instead you wait.
You let the rally attempt try to build for a few days.

And then, typically somewhere between the fourth and the seventh day of the attempted rally, you look for one powerful, decisive day where a major index surges up strongly, a big percentage gain, something on the order of one and a half percent or more, and critically, it does it on volume that's heavier than the day before.
That is the follow-through day.
It's the market's way of confirming, with conviction and with the big institutions now buying aggressively, that a real new uptrend has begun.
No new bull market in his hundred years of study ever started without a follow-through day.
Now, it's not perfect, occasionally a follow-through fails, but he found that essentially every genuine new uptrend was launched by one, so you never want to be betting big without having seen it.
The discipline this gives you is enormous.

You wait on the sidelines during the ugliness, you don't try to catch the exact bottom, you let the market prove it's turning by delivering a follow-through day, and then you go to work, because that's when your beautiful CAN SLIM stocks are breaking out of their bases and it's the right time to buy.
So the M ties everything together.

Read the indexes and their volume daily, respect the distribution days that warn of a top, wait for the follow-through day to confirm a new uptrend, and only be a heavy buyer when the market itself is with you.
So that's the full acronym, all seven letters.
C, current quarterly earnings up big and accelerating.
A, annual earnings growing strongly over years with a high return on equity.
N, something new in the company and a new high in the price out of a sound base.
S, supply and demand, tight share supply and big volume confirming the move.
L, buy the leader with high relative strength, never the cheap laggard.
I, growing institutional sponsorship from quality funds, but not an over-owned name.
And M, market direction, the most important of all, only buy heavily when the general market is in a confirmed uptrend.
That's the selection engine.
But knowing what to buy is only half the battle.
The other half, and honestly the half that separates the people who make money from the people who blow up, is knowing exactly when to buy and when to sell.
And that starts with reading charts.
Now, this is the part that's toughest to convey when you can't see a picture, so I'm going to slow way down and paint each pattern in words, carefully, so you can build the image in your mind.
The core concept is what O'Neil calls a base.

A base is a period where a stock, after a prior advance, pauses and consolidates, trading sideways in a range for a number of weeks or even months, working off its previous gains, shaking out the weak nervous holders, and building up a coiled foundation before, ideally, launching its next big move.
Learning to recognize these bases, and the precise point where the stock breaks out of them, is the chart-reading skill at the heart of the method.
There's a specific price within each base O'Neil calls the pivot point, or the buy point.
That pivot is the moment of maximum readiness, the price at which, if the stock pushes through it on huge volume, you buy.
Let me describe the three most important base patterns.
The first and most famous is the cup with handle.
Picture the letter U, or the silhouette of a coffee cup viewed from the side.
Here's how it forms.
The stock has had a good prior run up, then it starts to decline, rolling over and falling.
But it doesn't crash straight down, it curves down gradually, rounds out a bottom, and then curves back up gently, tracing that smooth U shape, that rounded cup.

This cup typically takes a while to form, often somewhere from about seven weeks to several months, and the depth of the cup, from the high on the left rim down to the bottom and back up, is usually somewhere in the range of around twelve to a third off the peak, roughly fifteen to thirty percent in a normal market, deeper in a wild bear market but you don't want it grotesquely deep.
So the stock climbs back up the right side of the cup until it's near the level of that old high on the left rim.
And here's where the handle forms.
Instead of blasting straight through the old high, the stock drifts sideways and slightly downward for a week or two, a small, gentle pullback near the top.
That's the handle, like the handle sticking off the right side of the coffee cup.

This handle is important, it should drift down gently on low, quiet volume, a final little shakeout of the last weak holders, and it should form in the upper half of the whole base, not down near the bottom.
The pivot, the buy point, is just above the peak of that handle, typically about ten cents above the highest price reached in the handle.

When the stock pushes up through that pivot point on a massive surge of volume, that huge volume we talked about in the S, forty, fifty percent or more above normal, that is the breakout, and that is O'Neil's classic moment to buy.
You're buying strength, buying the new high, buying exactly as the stock launches out of a proper, mature base with big money rushing in.
That cup with handle is the signature pattern of the book, the one that formed under many of history's greatest winners right before they took off.
The second pattern is the flat base.
This one is simpler.

A flat base is a stock that, often after already breaking out and advancing from an earlier base, goes sideways in a tight, relatively narrow horizontal band for a while, at least five or six weeks or more.
It doesn't correct much, the depth of the range is shallow, often only around ten to fifteen percent from top to bottom, much less than a cup.
It just trades flat, calmly digesting its prior gains, refusing to give back much ground, which is itself a sign of strength, strong stocks don't fall apart, they just rest.
The buy point on a flat base is just above the top of that tight sideways range, so when the stock pushes up out of the top of the flat band on a volume surge, that's your breakout.
Flat bases often form as a second-stage base after a stock has already had one run, and they can launch powerful further advances.
The third pattern is the double bottom.
Picture the letter W.

The stock declines, forming the first low, the first bottom, then it bounces up partway, forming the middle peak of the W, then it declines again, and here's the key characteristic, that second decline typically undercuts, dips slightly below, the price of the first low.

That little undercut is actually a good thing, it's a final shakeout that scares out and stops out the last of the weak holders who thought the first bottom would hold, flushing them out before the real move.
Then the stock recovers up the right side of the W.
In a double bottom, the pivot buy point is up near the top of the pattern, roughly at the level of that middle peak of the W, the high point between the two bottoms.
When the stock pushes through that pivot on strong volume, it's the breakout.
So the W-shaped double bottom is a close cousin of the cup, another sound base from which winners launch.
Across all these patterns, the common thread is the same.

You identify a proper base, you locate the pivot point, and you buy as the stock breaks out through that pivot on a dramatic surge of volume, ideally when the general market is in a confirmed uptrend.
And O'Neil gives you a discipline about how far to chase.
You want to buy right as it clears the pivot, in what he calls the buy zone, generally within about five percent above the pivot point.

If the stock has already screamed up fifteen, twenty percent past the pivot before you noticed, you've missed the ideal entry, it's extended, too late, too risky, and you should let it go and wait for the next opportunity rather than chasing it up there.
Discipline at the entry is everything, because your entry price is what your entire risk-control system is built around.
Which brings us to the most important part of the whole book.

If you remember nothing else from this entire drive, remember this next part, because O'Neil himself says the single most important thing in the whole method, the thing that determines whether you survive, is how you handle your losses.
And his rule is ironclad, mechanical, and non-negotiable.
Cut every single loss when the stock falls seven to eight percent below the price you paid for it.
Period.
No exceptions, no excuses, no hoping, no waiting for it to come back.
If you buy a stock at a hundred dollars and it drops to ninety-two or ninety-three, you sell it.
You are out.
You take the small loss and you move on, no matter how much you love the company, no matter how convinced you are that it'll bounce back, no matter what story you're telling yourself.
Seven to eight percent below your buy point, you're gone.
Now, why so strict?
Why be so unforgiving about it?
Because O'Neil understood something profound about both math and human nature.
On the math side, small losses are survivable, big losses are ruinous, and they're not symmetrical.
If you take an eight percent loss, you need less than a nine percent gain to make it back, no problem.
But if you let a loss run to fifty percent, because you kept hoping, now you need a hundred percent gain, a double, just to get back to even.
And if you let it fall further, the hole becomes mathematically almost impossible to climb out of.
One catastrophic loss can wipe out the gains from a dozen good decisions.
So the seven-to-eight-percent rule is your insurance policy.
It guarantees that no single mistake can ever take you out of the game.
And here's the beautiful part of the logic.
Since you're buying right at the pivot point of a proper base after a confirmed market follow-through, a correctly-bought stock generally should not fall seven or eight percent below that pivot.
If it does, something is wrong, your read was off, the market shifted, and the loss itself is the signal telling you to get out.
The small loss is information.
He points out you can be right just a third of the time and still make excellent money, as long as your losses are all kept small and tiny, and your winners are allowed to grow large.
Cut your losses short and let your winners run, the oldest wisdom in the market, and O'Neil gives you the exact number that makes it operational.
Eight percent, no exceptions.
And this leads directly to the rule he's most emphatic about on the losing side.
Never, ever average down.
Averaging down means buying more of a stock as it falls below your purchase price, to lower your average cost, telling yourself you're getting an even better bargain now.

O'Neil says this is one of the most dangerous things an amateur does, it's pouring more money into a losing position, into a stock the market is actively telling you is weak, and it violates everything, it violates cut your losses, it violates buy leaders and strength not laggards and weakness.
You add to your winners, the stocks that prove you right by going up, not to your losers.
If anything, when a stock is rising nicely from your buy point, you might buy a bit more early in the move, that's called averaging up, adding to strength.
But adding to a loser is throwing good money after bad, and it's how people turn a small manageable loss into an account-destroying disaster.
So burn it in.
Cut losses at eight percent, and never average down.
Okay, so that's the defensive side, protecting your capital.
Now the other side, the happier problem, when do you sell a winner and take your profit?
Because here's a truth that surprises people, selling correctly is harder than buying, and it's where a lot of the real money is won or lost.
O'Neil's general guideline on the upside is this.
In most cases, consider taking your profits when a stock is up around twenty to twenty-five percent from your buy point.
That sounds almost modest, doesn't it, when stocks can go up hundreds of percent, but there's deep logic to it.
Most breakouts advance something like twenty to twenty-five percent and then pull back and form a new base for a while before they go again.
So by taking that twenty to twenty-five percent gain, you're harvesting the meat of a typical move and avoiding giving it back during the correction.
And here's the asymmetry that makes the whole system work mathematically.
If you cut your losses at eight percent but you take your gains at twenty to twenty-five percent, then your average winner is roughly three times the size of your average loser.
With that ratio, you can be wrong more often than you're right and still come out well ahead.
That three-to-one relationship between your gains and your losses is the mathematical engine of the entire method.
But, and this is a crucial exception O'Neil carves out, there's a special kind of stock you do not sell at twenty to twenty-five percent.
Occasionally you'll catch one of the true, once-in-a-decade big winners, a genuine market leader in a powerful new uptrend that's destined to go up two hundred, three hundred, five hundred percent.
If you sold every one of those at twenty-five percent, you'd be clipping the wings off the very stocks that could make your whole year, your whole decade.
So O'Neil has a specific tell for identifying these.

If a stock races up twenty percent or more in just one, two, or three weeks after its breakout, blazingly fast, that extraordinary strength is a sign you may have a truly exceptional stock on your hands, and for those, he says hold on, give them at least eight weeks, and consider holding them for the long, big move rather than snatching the quick profit.
So the nuance is, take the normal twenty to twenty-five percent gain on your ordinary winners, but recognize and hold onto the rare rocket ships.

And to hold those big winners through their long advances without getting shaken out, he suggests using a moving average line, like the stock's ten-week average price, as a trailing guide, giving the stock room to breathe and only getting concerned if it breaks decisively below that line on heavy volume.
So how do you know when even a big winner is finally topping out and it's time to sell into the strength?

This is a genuine skill, and O'Neil gives you specific warning signs of a stock making a top, because the top usually comes while the stock is still going up and everything feels euphoric, which is exactly why most people sell too late.
Here are the tells to listen for.

One is climax action, where a stock that's been advancing for months suddenly goes vertical, spiking up faster and further in a week or two than at any point in its whole run, running up in a frenzy on huge volume.
That final blow-off frenzy, what he calls a climax top, often marks the exhaustion of the move, everyone who's going to buy has bought in a panic, and there's nobody left.
Counterintuitively, the most exciting, euphoric action is often the moment to sell, not buy.

Another sign is heavy selling volume with little price progress, days where the volume is enormous but the stock barely goes up or even closes down, telling you the big institutions are now distributing, unloading their shares into the excitement, that's distribution in your individual stock, the same concept as distribution days in the market.

Another is the stock breaking down through its key moving average on big volume after a long run, or forming later-stage, sloppy, wide, loose bases that keep failing, a fourth or fifth base is far more likely to fail than a first or second one.

And a broad one, when the leaders of the market start breaking down and the general market itself flashes those distribution days and rolls into a downtrend, that's your cue that it's time to be selling your stocks and moving to the safety of cash, because remember, three out of four stocks follow that market.
The overarching idea is to sell into strength while you still can, while there are eager buyers to sell to, rather than panicking on the way down with everyone else.
Let me pull the selling discipline together, because it has a clean logic.
On the downside, you have an absolute, mechanical, unbreakable rule, cut every loss at seven to eight percent, no thinking required, no emotion allowed.

On the upside, you have judgment, take your twenty to twenty-five percent gains on normal winners, but hold your rare rockets for the big move, and learn to read the topping signs so you can sell into strength near the peak.
And underneath all of it is that beautiful three-to-one asymmetry, small losses, bigger gains, which means you don't have to be right most of the time to win over the long run.
You just have to be disciplined every single time.

And that word, discipline, is really the soul of this whole book, so let's talk about the psychology, because O'Neil is adamant that the biggest obstacle between you and success isn't the market, it's you.
Your own emotions, your own ego, your own hope and fear and pride, are what will sink you if you let them.
He watched investor after investor sabotage themselves in the same predictable ways, and he wants you to see these traps clearly so you can resist them.
The number one killer is the refusal to take a small loss.

Somebody buys a stock, it drops eight percent, then twelve, then twenty, and at every step their pride and their hope won't let them admit they were wrong and sell, so they hold, and they pray, and a small manageable loss metastasizes into a devastating one.
They fall in love with the company, they marry the stock, they take it personally.

O'Neil says you cannot have any of that, the market does not know you own it and does not care what you paid, you have to be willing to be wrong quickly and cheaply, and admitting a mistake fast is a strength, not a weakness.
He calls out other emotional traps too.

People love to buy cheap, low-priced stocks because they can own more shares and it feels like more action, when the quality companies are usually the higher-priced ones, you're better off with a few shares of a great, expensive leader than a thousand shares of a cheap piece of junk.
People buy on tips and rumors and what their brother-in-law said and what some talking head on television recommended, instead of doing their own homework on the actual earnings and the actual chart.
People obsess over buying at the absolute bottom and bragging about their bargain, when the whole method says buy strength at new highs.
People let the fear of taxes stop them from selling a stock that should be sold, a foolish reason to hold a deteriorating position.
People anchor on the price they paid and can't act rationally from there.
And maybe the deepest one, people let hope substitute for a plan, they hope a loser comes back instead of following a rule, and they get fearful and sell a winner too early instead of letting it run.
O'Neil flips the emotions around on you.
When you should feel confident, buying a leader breaking out on huge volume in a strong market, it feels scary and too high.
And when you should feel scared, holding a falling loser, it feels comfortable and hopeful.
His entire system is built to force you to override those backwards instincts with objective, pre-decided rules.
The rules exist precisely so that in the heat of the moment, when your emotions are screaming at you to do the wrong thing, you can fall back on the discipline instead of the feeling.
That's why the eight percent rule is a number and not a judgment call.
It takes the emotion out.
He's also refreshingly humble and empirical about the whole thing.

He tells you to keep records of your own trades, to review your winners and especially your losers, and to actually study what you did right and wrong, so that you're constantly learning from your own real behavior, not just repeating the same mistakes blindly.
He wants you to do post-analysis, to draw lessons, to improve.
The market is the ultimate teacher if you're willing to study your own report card honestly.

And he grounds all of it in humility toward history, everything he teaches came from studying what actually worked in the real market across a hundred years, not from theory or opinion or what should work.

That's why he built those model books of past winners, and why he founded Investor's Business Daily, to hand ordinary people the same factual data, the earnings ratings and relative strength ratings and the tools to read the market's direction, that the professionals had kept to themselves.
His whole stance is, don't argue with the market, don't have opinions the market doesn't confirm, follow the rules that history and price and volume are showing you.
So let me bring this all home and give you the disciplined rules to carry away, the ones to keep turning over in your mind after this drive is over.
First, use CAN SLIM as your checklist for what to even consider buying.
C, current quarterly earnings up big, ideally twenty-five percent or much more, and accelerating.
A, a strong multi-year record of annual earnings growth, twenty-five percent a year or better, with a return on equity of at least seventeen percent.

N, a company with something genuinely new driving it, a new product, service, management, or industry condition, and a stock breaking out to new highs, because what looks too high usually goes higher and what looks cheap usually goes lower.
S, supply and demand, favor reasonable share supply and demand you can see confirmed by a huge volume surge on the breakout.
L, buy the true leader with a relative strength rating of eighty or higher, never the cheap sympathetic laggard.
I, look for growing institutional sponsorship from quality funds, but avoid the over-owned, played-out name.

And M, the most important, only buy aggressively when the general market is in a confirmed uptrend, reading the daily indexes and their volume, respecting distribution days as warnings and waiting for a follow-through day to confirm a new uptrend.
Second, buy at the right point.

Learn to spot the bases, the cup with handle with its rounded U and its gentle drifting handle near the top, the tight sideways flat base, the W-shaped double bottom, and buy right as the stock clears its pivot point on a volume surge forty to fifty percent or more above normal, staying within about five percent of that pivot and never chasing a stock that's already run away from you.
Third, and above all, manage your risk with iron discipline.
Cut every single loss at seven to eight percent below what you paid, mechanically, no exceptions, no hoping, because small losses keep you alive and one big loss can end you.
Never, ever average down into a losing stock.

Take most of your profits in the twenty to twenty-five percent range so your winners run about three times the size of your losses, but hold onto the rare, explosive market leaders for the big move, and learn to sell into strength when you see a climax top or heavy distribution rather than riding it all the way down.

And master your own psychology, because your emotions will always tempt you to do the exact opposite of the right thing, to hold losers out of hope and sell winners out of fear, and the whole point of these rules is to protect you from yourself.
That, in its essence, is William O'Neil's CAN SLIM.

A selection engine built from a hundred years of the market's greatest winners, wrapped around a risk-management discipline so strict that it keeps you in the game long enough for the winners to pay for the losers many times over.
It rewards the patient, the objective, and the disciplined, and it punishes the emotional, the stubborn, and the hopeful.
O'Neil's promise was never that you'd be right every time, in fact he assumed you'd be wrong plenty.
His promise was that if you follow the rules, keep your losses tiny, let your winners run, and only fight when the market is on your side, then over time the math and the method will carry you.
Keep the seven letters in your head, keep the eight percent rule in your bones, and remember that in this game, protecting your capital always comes first.
Drive safe, and good luck out there.
